text.content:
Downtime projects give heroes personal goals to pursue between adventures. Track project points, manage prerequisites, and use events to inject drama into their crafting and research.

Project Basics

Each project has four elements you need to track:

Element Description
Item Prerequisite A special item or component the hero must possess before starting. Some projects have none.
Project Source Lore guiding the work — a book, tutor, schematic, inscription. The hero must have access each time they make a project roll. You choose the language.
Project Goal The total project points needed to complete the project.
Project Roll The characteristic used for the roll (Might, Reason, Intuition, or Presence). No tiers — the total rolled is the points earned (minimum 1).

Heroes can start as many projects as they wish, but can only work on one per respite. Other heroes or NPCs can contribute project rolls using their own respite activity.

Project Roll Modifiers

Modifier Effect
Applicable Skill +2 (crafting or lore skills, or others at your discretion if directly relevant).
Edge / Double Edge +2 / +4 to the roll.
Bane / Double Bane −2 / −4 to the roll.
Language: Known No penalty.
Language: Related Bane on the roll.
Language: Unknown Double bane on the roll.
Breakthrough (nat 19–20) Earn points as normal, then make another project roll immediately (same respite activity).

The minimum result on a project roll is always 1, even with penalties.

Project Sources

What counts as a project source? A book, scroll, schematic, expert tutor, master artisan, supernatural manifestation, or any other form of lore you determine. A hero can serve as a project source for another hero, but must use their respite activity to do so.

Finding common sources: If a source is common enough to exist in a substantial library or among local experts, a hero can locate it as a respite activity the next time they rest somewhere with such access.

Language: You decide the language. In Svellheim, project sources are commonly in Svellspraak, Dvergsmål, or the tongue of the relevant culture. Rare or ancient sources might be in a dead or foreign language to create an adventure hook.

Discover Lore (Research Projects)

When a hero wants to research something that can't be resolved with a single test, use the Discover Lore project. The hero describes what they want to know, and you determine the knowledge type:

Knowledge Type Goal Description
Common 15 Easy to find with a day of research — rumour tracing, local customs, religious references. Plentiful local sources.
Obscure 45 Known only to specialists. Requires several days of focused work. Few tomes cover this.
Lost 120 Maybe one or two people in the world know this. The lore may exist in a single text in a dead language. Takes more than a week.
Forbidden 240 Actively concealed by a powerful individual or faction. Encoded, whispered, protected. Weeks of work.

Research projects use Reason by default. Item prerequisite: none (unless the lore requires examining something specific). Project source: texts or knowledge related to the subject.

Find a Cure

Characteristic: Reason or Intuition

Item Prerequisite: Remains of the creature that caused the affliction (or one of the same kind).

Project Goal: 50 × the creature's level. For environmental or supernatural afflictions, you set the prerequisite and goal.

Completion: Creates one dose of an alchemical cure. Subsequent doses cost half the original project goal.

Crafting Projects — Quick Reference

Project Goal Characteristic Key Prerequisite
Craft Treasure Varies Varies Per treasure entry
Imbue Armor (1st) 150 Might / Reason / Intuition Per enhancement + mundane item
Imbue Weapon (1st) 150 Might / Reason / Intuition Per enhancement + mundane item
Build Airship 3,000 Might / Reason / Presence Wind Crystal of Quintessence
Build / Repair Road 10 × miles Might / Reason / Presence 3 guild writs of approval
Craft Teleportation Platform 1,500 Reason Spatial navigator
Find a Cure 50 × level Reason / Intuition Creature remains

Imbuing Treasures

Heroes can imbue a mundane item up to 3 times:

  • 1st Imbuing: Pick a 1st-level enhancement. Armor also grants +6 Stamina.
  • 2nd Imbuing: Pick a 5th-level enhancement. Armor Stamina bonus increases to +12.
  • 3rd Imbuing: Pick a 9th-level enhancement. Armor Stamina bonus increases to +21.

A hero must have a kit that includes the item type and their level must match the enhancement's level. Numbered enhancements (e.g., Phasing III) require all previous versions first.

Project Events

Project events add drama and narrative to the research/crafting process. They are entirely optional. Choose one of three methods to trigger events:

Method How It Works
Roll for Event Once during any respite with a project roll, roll a d6. On a 6, an event occurs. Good if you improvise easily.
Event Milestones Events trigger at fixed point thresholds (see table below). Good for planning ahead.
Whenever You Want Deploy events at the most dramatic moment. Good for maximum narrative control.

Suggested Event Milestones

Project Goal Events
30 or fewer None
31–200 1 event at halfway to the goal
201–999 2 events at ⅓ and ⅔ of the goal
1,000+ 3 events at ¼, ½, and ¾ of the goal

Automatic Breakthrough (from events): The project gains 20 project points and the hero can make another project roll as part of the same respite activity.

Events occur either before or after the project roll, as noted. They can grant bonus points, impose additional costs, introduce NPC complications, or create adventure hooks. Use the event tables in the rules, or invent your own using these prompts as inspiration.

Guides

A guide is a book, schematic, or NPC that grants a specific number of project points toward a project without a roll. The hero studies the guide as a respite activity. Each guide can only be used once per project.

  • Full knowledge value if the hero knows the guide's language.
  • Half knowledge value (rounded down) if the hero only knows a related language.

Award guides to accelerate complex projects in campaigns with limited downtime. A guide worth 1,000+ points for a 3,000-point project dramatically shortens the crafting timeline.

Quick Setup Checklist

  1. When a hero proposes a project, determine the item prerequisite and project source (including language).
  2. Set the project goal (use Discover Lore table for research, or the specific project's listed goal for crafting).
  3. Confirm which characteristic the hero rolls with and which skills apply.
  4. Check whether any language penalties (bane or double bane) apply.
  5. Decide your event method — random (d6), milestone, or narrative.
  6. During each respite, note which heroes work on projects. Resolve rolls and track accumulated points.
  7. When project points ≥ project goal, the project is complete. Narrate the result.

Director Tips

  • Make sources into hooks. The search for a project source can be as exciting as the project itself. Put the schematic in a dragon's hoard, the expert behind enemy lines, the ritual instructions in a dead language.
  • Pace with the campaign. Short campaigns need lower goals or generous guides. Long campaigns can use big goals and let projects unfold naturally.
  • Custom projects welcome. If a hero wants to build a longship, establish a spy network, or train a beast companion, design a project for it — pick a prerequisite, source, characteristic, and goal.
  • Events drive story. A thief targeting a project source, a supernatural patron offering a shortcut, a guild refusing to cooperate — these events make downtime memorable.
  • Collaboration matters. Remind players they can help each other. The fury holds the forge while the tactician reads the schematic — both contribute project rolls.
  • Don't forget career points. Some heroes start with 120 or 240 project points from their career. Ask during character creation what they spent them on.
